thrombolysis.name: NINDS
thrombolysis.question: Does IV alteplase within 3h of symptom onset improve outcomes in acute ischemic stroke?
thrombolysis.design: RCT, n=624, alteplase 0.9 mg/kg vs placebo within 3h
thrombolysis.outcomes: Favorable outcome (mRS 0-1) at 3mo: 39% vs 26%. sICH: 6.4% vs 0.6%.
thrombolysis.bottomLine: IV alteplase within 3h improves neurologic recovery despite bleeding risk. Foundation of modern stroke treatment.
thrombolysis.teachingPoint: Time is brain — NNT drops from ~5 at 90 min to ~14 by 3h. "Door-to-needle" <45 min is AHA benchmark.

thrombolysis.name: ECASS III
thrombolysis.question: Is IV alteplase effective 3-4.5h after symptom onset?
thrombolysis.design: RCT, n=821, alteplase vs placebo in 3-4.5h window
thrombolysis.bottomLine: Extended the window to 4.5h with similar NNT/NNH profile.
thrombolysis.teachingPoint: ECASS III exclusions (age >80, NIHSS >25, DM + prior stroke, any AC) were NOT carried forward to AHA 2019 guidelines for the 0-3h window.

thrombolysis.name: EXTEND-IA TNK
thrombolysis.question: Is TNK non-inferior to alteplase before EVT for LVO?
thrombolysis.design: RCT, n=202, TNK 0.25 mg/kg vs alteplase 0.9 mg/kg before EVT
thrombolysis.outcomes: Substantial reperfusion: 22% vs 10% (P=0.002). mRS 0-2 at 90d: 64% vs 51%.
thrombolysis.bottomLine: TNK superior to alteplase for early reperfusion in LVO.
thrombolysis.teachingPoint: TNK is non-inferior to alteplase for AIS and may be superior for LVO. Single bolus (vs 1-hr infusion) → logistic advantage for drip-and-ship.

thrombolysis.name: AcT
thrombolysis.question: Is TNK 0.25 mg/kg non-inferior to alteplase 0.9 mg/kg in general AIS?
thrombolysis.design: RCT, n=1577, Canadian multicenter, ≤4.5h from onset
thrombolysis.outcomes: mRS 0-1 at 90-120d: 36.9% (TNK) vs 34.8% (alteplase), meeting non-inferiority. sICH: 3.4% vs 3.2%.
thrombolysis.bottomLine: TNK is non-inferior to alteplase in real-world AIS. Simpler single-bolus administration.
thrombolysis.teachingPoint: TNK has replaced alteplase as first-line at many centers due to simpler bolus-only dosing and comparable efficacy/safety.

thrombolysis.name: WAKE-UP
thrombolysis.question: Does alteplase benefit patients with unknown-onset stroke if DWI-FLAIR mismatch is present?
thrombolysis.design: RCT, n=503, alteplase vs placebo with DWI positive + FLAIR negative (implies <4.5h from onset)
thrombolysis.bottomLine: DWI-FLAIR mismatch identifies wake-up stroke patients within "tissue window" for lysis.
thrombolysis.teachingPoint: Tissue-based selection (imaging) can replace time-based selection when LKW is unknown. Foundation of extended-window treatment.

thrombolysis.name: EXTEND
thrombolysis.question: Does alteplase benefit patients 4.5-9h from onset with CTP/MRP mismatch?
thrombolysis.design: RCT, n=225, alteplase vs placebo with perfusion mismatch (core <70 mL, ratio ≥1.2, penumbra ≥10 mL)
thrombolysis.outcomes: mRS 0-1 at 90d: 35% vs 29%, aOR 1.44. sICH: 6% vs 1%.
thrombolysis.bottomLine: Perfusion mismatch extends lytic window to 9h in selected patients.
thrombolysis.teachingPoint: The imaging criteria (core <70, ratio ≥1.2, mismatch ≥10) came directly from EXTEND and are now the standard for extended-window selection.

thrombolysis.name: TIMELESS
thrombolysis.question: Does TNK 4.5-24h improve outcomes in LVO stroke with salvageable tissue proceeding to EVT?
thrombolysis.design: RCT, n=458, TNK vs placebo pre-EVT in 4.5-24h
thrombolysis.outcomes: mRS shift at 90d: not significant (primary neutral). Excellent outcome (mRS 0-1): 46% vs 42%, no significant difference.
thrombolysis.bottomLine: Neutral for pre-EVT TNK beyond 4.5h; safe but no efficacy benefit demonstrated.
thrombolysis.teachingPoint: Bridging IVT before EVT beyond 4.5h did NOT show benefit; EVT alone may be sufficient in late window.

thrombolysis.name: TRACE-2
thrombolysis.question: Is TNK non-inferior to alteplase in Chinese AIS patients ineligible for EVT?
thrombolysis.design: RCT, n=1430, TNK vs alteplase ≤4.5h, Chinese population
thrombolysis.bottomLine: TNK non-inferior to alteplase in non-LVO AIS. Confirms AcT findings in different population.
thrombolysis.teachingPoint: TRACE-2 + AcT establish TNK as standard-of-care alternative to alteplase across populations.

thrombolysis.name: TWIST
thrombolysis.question: Does TNK benefit wake-up stroke patients without advanced imaging selection?
thrombolysis.design: RCT, n=578, TNK vs standard care for wake-up stroke (no CTP/MRI required)
thrombolysis.outcomes: Excellent outcome (mRS 0): no significant difference. Ordinal shift: no benefit.
thrombolysis.bottomLine: Without advanced imaging selection, TNK did not improve outcomes in wake-up stroke.
thrombolysis.teachingPoint: Imaging selection (DWI-FLAIR mismatch or CTP) is essential for benefit in extended-window lytics. "Just treat based on clock + CT" does not work in wake-up.

thrombectomy.name: MR CLEAN
thrombectomy.question: Does EVT improve outcomes in acute anterior circulation LVO within 6h?
thrombectomy.design: RCT, n=500, stent retriever + usual care vs usual care (majority received tPA)
thrombectomy.outcomes: mRS 0-2 at 90d: 33% vs 19%, aOR 2.16. sICH: 8% vs 6%.
thrombectomy.bottomLine: First unequivocally positive EVT trial, launching the modern thrombectomy era.
thrombectomy.teachingPoint: Pooled HERMES analysis of MR CLEAN + ESCAPE + EXTEND-IA + SWIFT PRIME + REVASCAT showed NNT 2.6 for improvement and NNT 5 for functional independence at 90d.

thrombectomy.name: ESCAPE
thrombectomy.question: Does rapid EVT with imaging selection benefit LVO within 12h?
thrombectomy.design: RCT, n=316, EVT vs usual care with small core + good collaterals on CTA
thrombectomy.outcomes: mRS 0-2 at 90d: 53% vs 29%. NNT 4 for functional independence.
thrombectomy.teachingPoint: Good collaterals on CTA correlate with small core and viable penumbra — critical for late-window decisions.

thrombectomy.name: EXTEND-IA
thrombectomy.question: Does EVT benefit perfusion-selected LVO patients?
thrombectomy.design: RCT, n=70, CTP selection with core <70 mL, mismatch ratio >1.2, absolute mismatch >10 mL
thrombectomy.outcomes: Early neurologic improvement: 80% vs 37%. mRS 0-2 at 90d: 71% vs 40%.
thrombectomy.bottomLine: Perfusion imaging identifies patients with the greatest benefit from EVT.
thrombectomy.teachingPoint: EXTEND-IA validated perfusion-selected EVT. Its >1.2 / >10 mL mismatch profile later aligned with EXTEND IVT; DAWN used clinical-core mismatch, while DEFUSE-3 used stricter perfusion mismatch (ratio ≥1.8, mismatch ≥15 mL).

thrombectomy.name: SWIFT PRIME
thrombectomy.question: Does stent retriever EVT improve outcomes after IV tPA?
thrombectomy.design: RCT, n=196, tPA + EVT vs tPA alone
thrombectomy.bottomLine: Confirmed EVT benefit on top of IV tPA.
thrombectomy.teachingPoint: Bridging thrombolysis + EVT was standard until trials questioned whether EVT alone might be non-inferior in facility settings.

thrombectomy.name: DAWN
thrombectomy.question: Does EVT 6-24h from LKW benefit patients with clinical-core mismatch?
thrombectomy.design: RCT, n=206, tier-based inclusion (age, NIHSS, core volume)
thrombectomy.outcomes: mRS 0-2 at 90d: 49% vs 13%. NNT 2.8 for functional independence.
thrombectomy.bottomLine: Extended the EVT window to 24h with tissue-based selection.
thrombectomy.teachingPoint: DAWN tiers (A: ≥80yo + NIHSS ≥10 + core <21; B: <80 + NIHSS ≥10 + core <31; C: <80 + NIHSS ≥20 + core <51) — know by heart for telestroke calls.

thrombectomy.name: DEFUSE-3
thrombectomy.question: Does EVT 6-16h benefit CTP-selected patients?
thrombectomy.design: RCT, n=182, core ≤70 mL + mismatch ratio ≥1.8 + mismatch volume ≥15 mL
thrombectomy.teachingPoint: DEFUSE-3 core/ratio/mismatch criteria are easier to memorize than DAWN tiers; both are Class 1A in AHA/ASA guidelines.

thrombectomy.name: SELECT2
thrombectomy.question: Does EVT benefit patients with LARGE ischemic core (ASPECTS 3-5 or core 50-100 mL)?
thrombectomy.design: RCT, n=352, EVT vs medical management in 0-24h with large core
thrombectomy.bottomLine: EVT improves outcomes even with large core — the "too big to treat" paradigm was wrong.
thrombectomy.teachingPoint: Consider EVT in large-core patients with pre-stroke independence; counsel honestly about likelihood of moderate disability even with successful recanalization.

thrombectomy.name: ANGEL-ASPECT
thrombectomy.question: Does EVT benefit Chinese patients with ASPECTS 3-5?
thrombectomy.design: RCT, n=456, EVT vs medical management, 6-24h
thrombectomy.bottomLine: Confirms SELECT2 in a different population.
thrombectomy.teachingPoint: ANGEL-ASPECT + SELECT2 + RESCUE-Japan LIMIT together establish large-core EVT as standard of care.

thrombectomy.name: RESCUE-Japan LIMIT
thrombectomy.question: Does EVT benefit patients with ASPECTS 3-5?
thrombectomy.bottomLine: Early large-core trial establishing EVT benefit in ASPECTS 3-5.
thrombectomy.teachingPoint: Primary endpoint was mRS 0-3 (not 0-2) — relevant when counseling, as "functional independence" is set lower in large-core populations.

thrombectomy.name: BAOCHE + ATTENTION
thrombectomy.question: Does EVT benefit basilar artery occlusion?
thrombectomy.design: Two separate RCTs in Chinese populations
thrombectomy.outcomes: mRS 0-3 at 90d: substantially higher with EVT. Mortality reduced.
thrombectomy.bottomLine: Basilar artery EVT improves outcomes in severely affected patients (NIHSS ≥10).
thrombectomy.teachingPoint: BASICS (2021) was neutral; BAOCHE + ATTENTION positive. Reconcile: select patients with PC-ASPECTS ≥6, NIHSS ≥10, early presentation.

postEVTbp.name: ENCHANTED2-MT
postEVTbp.question: Does more intensive SBP lowering (<120 vs <140) improve outcomes post-EVT?
postEVTbp.design: RCT, n=821, intensive (<120) vs less-intensive (140-180) SBP control for 72h post successful EVT
postEVTbp.outcomes: Primary (mRS shift at 90d): worse in intensive (common OR 1.37 for poor outcome). Early neurologic deterioration more common.
postEVTbp.bottomLine: Intensive SBP lowering (<120) post-EVT is HARMFUL. Standard target SBP <180.
postEVTbp.teachingPoint: Why current guidance is SBP <180 (not <140) post-successful EVT. Extrapolation: maintain SBP floor of 140 for 72h.

postEVTbp.name: OPTIMAL-BP
postEVTbp.question: Does intensive SBP <140 improve outcomes post-EVT vs SBP <180?
postEVTbp.design: RCT, n=306, Korean population, intensive (<140) vs standard (<180) for 24h
postEVTbp.outcomes: mRS 0-2 at 90d: 39.4% vs 54.4% (worse with intensive). Trial stopped early for futility/harm.
postEVTbp.bottomLine: Confirms ENCHANTED2-MT — intensive BP lowering post-EVT is NOT beneficial and likely harmful.
postEVTbp.teachingPoint: OPTIMAL-BP stopped early for harm at n=306. Post-EVT BP target: SBP <180, not <140.

postEVTbp.name: BP-TARGET
postEVTbp.question: Does SBP 100-129 improve outcomes post-EVT vs SBP 130-185?
postEVTbp.design: RCT, n=324, France, intensive vs standard post-successful EVT
postEVTbp.outcomes: Primary (radiographic ICH at 24-36h): no significant difference. Symptomatic ICH higher with intensive.
postEVTbp.bottomLine: No benefit to intensive BP lowering post-EVT; trend toward harm.
postEVTbp.teachingPoint: BP-TARGET + ENCHANTED2-MT + OPTIMAL-BP + BEST-II converge on the same answer: post-EVT SBP should be in the 140-180 range.

postEVTbp.name: BEST-II
postEVTbp.question: Are more aggressive SBP targets (<140, <130) better than <180 post-EVT?
postEVTbp.outcomes: Lower targets trended toward worse outcomes; non-futility not demonstrated.
postEVTbp.bottomLine: Seals the case — no benefit to SBP targets below 140 post-EVT.
postEVTbp.teachingPoint: Consistent with prior trials. Standard post-EVT target SBP <180, avoid <140 for 72h.

ichReversal.name: INTERACT2
ichReversal.question: Does intensive BP lowering (SBP <140) improve outcomes in acute ICH?
ichReversal.design: RCT, n=2839, intensive (SBP <140 within 1h) vs standard (<180)
ichReversal.outcomes: Primary (mRS 3-6 at 90d): no significant difference. Ordinal analysis favored intensive.
ichReversal.bottomLine: Intensive BP lowering to SBP 140 is safe and may be beneficial; not harmful in ICH.
ichReversal.teachingPoint: Basis for 2022 AHA/ASA recommendation: target SBP 140 (range 130-150) for presenting SBP 150-220.

ichReversal.name: ATACH-2
ichReversal.question: Does aggressive BP lowering (SBP 110-139) improve outcomes vs standard (140-179)?
ichReversal.design: RCT, n=1000, aggressive vs standard with nicardipine
ichReversal.outcomes: Primary outcome: no difference. Renal adverse events: 9% vs 4% (higher in aggressive).
ichReversal.bottomLine: Going BELOW SBP 130 is not beneficial and may cause harm.
ichReversal.teachingPoint: Why the 2022 guideline says "avoid SBP <130 — Class 3: Harm" in ICH.

ichReversal.name: INTERACT3
ichReversal.question: Does a care bundle (early intensive BP, glucose, temp, AC reversal) improve ICH outcomes?
ichReversal.design: Cluster RCT, n=7036, bundle vs usual care
ichReversal.outcomes: Ordinal shift mRS at 6mo favored bundle (OR 0.86). Mortality: trend toward benefit.
ichReversal.bottomLine: A bundle of early intensive BP + glucose + fever + AC reversal improves ICH outcomes.
ichReversal.teachingPoint: INTERACT3 is about the BUNDLE, not BP alone. Each component reinforces the others.

ichReversal.name: MISTIE III
ichReversal.question: Does minimally-invasive clot lysis (alteplase through catheter) improve ICH outcomes?
ichReversal.design: RCT, n=506, MIS+alteplase vs medical management for 30-100 mL ICH
ichReversal.outcomes: Primary (mRS 0-3 at 1yr): no overall benefit. Per-protocol subgroup (residual clot <15 mL): mRS 0-3 significantly improved.
ichReversal.bottomLine: Technical success (clot reduction to <15 mL) correlates with outcome; overall neutral trial.
ichReversal.teachingPoint: Set up the ENRICH trial (positive) and current trend toward minimally-invasive evacuation for selected ICH.

ichReversal.name: ENRICH
ichReversal.question: Does minimally-invasive parafascicular surgery (BrainPath) improve lobar ICH outcomes?
ichReversal.design: RCT, n=300, MIPS+medical vs medical for lobar ICH 30-80 mL
ichReversal.outcomes: Primary (utility-weighted mRS at 180d): significantly favored MIPS.
ichReversal.bottomLine: First positive ICH surgery trial — MIPS improves outcomes in selected lobar ICH patients.
ichReversal.teachingPoint: June 2026 ICH algorithm screen: lobar ICH 30-80 mL, NIHSS >5, GCS 5-14, age 18-80, and no underlying lesion. Deep ICH and cerebellar ICH were excluded; verify operative timing and detailed trial criteria against the active protocol before applying.

ichReversal.name: ANNEXA-4 / ANNEXA-I
ichReversal.question: Does andexanet alfa reduce hematoma expansion in factor Xa inhibitor-associated ICH?
ichReversal.design: Single-arm (ANNEXA-4) → RCT (ANNEXA-I): andexanet vs usual care
ichReversal.outcomes: ANNEXA-I: hemostatic efficacy 67% vs 53%. Thrombotic events: 10.3% vs 5.6%.
ichReversal.bottomLine: Effective at reducing expansion but higher thrombotic risk; individualized decision.
ichReversal.teachingPoint: PCC remains first-line for factor Xa-associated ICH at many centers given cost/access of andexanet and thrombotic signal. 2022 AHA/ASA ICH guideline gives andexanet a Class 2a recommendation.

secondaryPrevention.name: CHANCE
secondaryPrevention.question: Does DAPT (clopidogrel + ASA) reduce stroke after minor stroke/TIA?
secondaryPrevention.design: RCT, n=5170, Chinese, clopi+ASA x 21d vs ASA alone
secondaryPrevention.bottomLine: Short-course DAPT reduces early recurrence after minor stroke/high-risk TIA.
secondaryPrevention.teachingPoint: Inclusion: NIHSS ≤3 or ABCD² ≥4. DAPT duration 21 days (longer duration increases bleeding).

secondaryPrevention.name: POINT
secondaryPrevention.question: Does DAPT reduce stroke in minor stroke/TIA (US/international)?
secondaryPrevention.design: RCT, n=4881, clopi+ASA planned x90d; halted early for lower ischemic events but higher major hemorrhage
secondaryPrevention.outcomes: 90-day major ischemic events: 5.0% vs 6.5%, HR 0.75. Major bleeding: 0.9% vs 0.4%.
secondaryPrevention.bottomLine: DAPT reduces ischemic events but increases bleeding. Optimal duration appears to be 21 days (Kim et al. 2019 meta-analysis).
secondaryPrevention.teachingPoint: Take-home: DAPT for 21 days after minor stroke (NIHSS ≤3) or high-risk TIA (ABCD² ≥4), then single antiplatelet.

secondaryPrevention.name: THALES
secondaryPrevention.question: Does ticagrelor + ASA reduce stroke/death in minor-to-moderate AIS or high-risk TIA?
secondaryPrevention.design: RCT, n=11,016, ticagrelor+ASA x 30d vs ASA alone
secondaryPrevention.outcomes: 30-day stroke or death: 5.5% vs 6.6%, HR 0.83. Severe bleeding: 0.5% vs 0.1%.
secondaryPrevention.bottomLine: Ticagrelor+ASA effective in up to NIHSS 5 or high-risk TIA with atherosclerosis.
secondaryPrevention.teachingPoint: Inclusion includes NIHSS ≤5 (higher than CHANCE/POINT). Useful when CYP2C19 LOF status known (ticagrelor not affected).

secondaryPrevention.name: CHANCE-2
secondaryPrevention.question: In CYP2C19 LOF carriers, is ticagrelor+ASA better than clopidogrel+ASA?
secondaryPrevention.design: RCT, n=6412, ticagrelor+ASA vs clopi+ASA x 21d in CYP2C19 LOF carriers
secondaryPrevention.bottomLine: In CYP2C19 LOF carriers, switch to ticagrelor.
secondaryPrevention.teachingPoint: CYP2C19 LOF (≈30% of patients, higher in Asian populations) → impaired clopidogrel activation. Point-of-care genotyping is increasingly available.

secondaryPrevention.name: SPARCL
secondaryPrevention.question: Does atorvastatin 80 mg reduce recurrent stroke in non-cardioembolic stroke/TIA?
secondaryPrevention.design: RCT, n=4731, atorvastatin 80 vs placebo, LDL ~130 at baseline
secondaryPrevention.teachingPoint: Small increase in hemorrhagic stroke (2.3% vs 1.4%) but overall net benefit. Informed later TST trial showing LDL <70 target.

secondaryPrevention.name: TST (Treat Stroke to Target)
secondaryPrevention.question: Is LDL <70 better than 90-110 for secondary stroke prevention?
secondaryPrevention.bottomLine: LDL <70 is the target for secondary stroke prevention.
secondaryPrevention.teachingPoint: LDL target <70 is now standard; if not achieved on high-intensity statin, add ezetimibe, then PCSK9i per 2021 AHA/ASA guideline.

secondaryPrevention.name: ARCADIA
secondaryPrevention.question: Does apixaban reduce recurrent stroke in ESUS patients with atrial cardiopathy markers?
secondaryPrevention.design: RCT, n=1015, apixaban vs ASA in ESUS with NT-proBNP ≥250 or LA enlargement
secondaryPrevention.outcomes: Primary: no significant difference. Recurrent stroke: 4.4% vs 4.7%.
secondaryPrevention.bottomLine: Empiric anticoagulation in ESUS with atrial cardiopathy markers does NOT reduce recurrence.
secondaryPrevention.teachingPoint: Joins NAVIGATE-ESUS, RE-SPECT ESUS in showing empiric AC does not work for ESUS. Search for PAF with loop recorders instead.

secondaryPrevention.name: NAVIGATE-ESUS + RE-SPECT ESUS
secondaryPrevention.question: Does rivaroxaban or dabigatran reduce recurrence in ESUS?
secondaryPrevention.design: Two large RCTs of DOAC vs ASA in ESUS
secondaryPrevention.outcomes: Both neutral for efficacy; higher bleeding with AC.
secondaryPrevention.bottomLine: Empiric AC in ESUS does not help. Find the etiology.
secondaryPrevention.teachingPoint: ESUS is a heterogeneous population — some have atrial cardiopathy, some aortic plaque, some PFO, some occult AF. Empiric AC treats none optimally.

secondaryPrevention.name: CLOSE / RESPECT / REDUCE
secondaryPrevention.question: Does PFO closure reduce recurrent stroke in patients with cryptogenic stroke + PFO?
secondaryPrevention.design: Three RCTs, closure vs medical management
secondaryPrevention.outcomes: All favored closure, particularly in patients with large shunt or atrial septal aneurysm.
secondaryPrevention.bottomLine: PFO closure recommended in select cryptogenic stroke patients age <60 with high-risk PFO features.
secondaryPrevention.teachingPoint: Use RoPE score to assess likelihood PFO is causal. RoPE ≥7 + high-risk features → closure (Class 1).

secondaryPrevention.name: CADISS / TREAT-CAD
secondaryPrevention.question: Is aspirin non-inferior to VKA for cervical artery dissection?
secondaryPrevention.design: Two RCTs, ASA vs VKA, 3-6 months
secondaryPrevention.outcomes: CADISS: non-inferior but underpowered. TREAT-CAD: ASA non-inferior for primary endpoint.
secondaryPrevention.bottomLine: Aspirin non-inferior to warfarin for cervical dissection.
secondaryPrevention.teachingPoint: Aspirin is first-line; VKA/DOAC reserved for recurrent events on ASA or free-floating thrombus.

secondaryPrevention.name: CREST / CREST-2
secondaryPrevention.question: CEA vs CAS for symptomatic carotid stenosis; CAS vs medical management for asymptomatic
secondaryPrevention.design: CREST: CEA vs CAS. CREST-2: CEA+IMM vs IMM alone; CAS+IMM vs IMM alone.
secondaryPrevention.outcomes: CREST: similar primary outcomes, CAS has more peri-procedural strokes, CEA more MIs. CREST-2: modern medical management may be as good as revascularization.
secondaryPrevention.bottomLine: For asymptomatic ≥70% stenosis: intensive medical management is an evidence-based alternative to revascularization.
secondaryPrevention.teachingPoint: CREST-2 challenges decades of asymptomatic carotid management — intensive medical therapy (LDL <70, SBP <130, antiplatelet, smoking cessation) may obviate need for CEA/CAS.

antithrombotic.name: RE-LY / ROCKET-AF / ARISTOTLE / ENGAGE AF
antithrombotic.question: Are DOACs non-inferior or superior to warfarin for AF stroke prevention?
antithrombotic.outcomes: All DOACs non-inferior or superior. Less ICH across all DOACs.
antithrombotic.bottomLine: DOACs are first-line over warfarin for non-valvular AF.
antithrombotic.teachingPoint: Apixaban (ARISTOTLE) had superior mortality + less bleeding; preferred at many centers. Dose adjust for age ≥80, weight ≤60, creatinine ≥1.5 (2 of 3: reduce to 2.5 mg BID).

antithrombotic.name: ELAN
antithrombotic.question: When to start DOAC after AF-related ischemic stroke — early vs late?
antithrombotic.design: RCT, n=2013, early DOAC using ELAN imaging-defined infarct size vs later (day 3/6/12-14) initiation
antithrombotic.outcomes: 30-day recurrence/ICH/death composite: 2.9% early vs 4.1% late (95% CI favoring early). No excess ICH.
antithrombotic.bottomLine: Early DOAC initiation by ELAN imaging severity showed no excess ICH versus later start and supports earlier treatment in selected AF-stroke patients.
antithrombotic.teachingPoint: ELAN classified stroke severity by imaging-defined infarct size/territory, not NIHSS cutoffs. NIHSS-based buckets can be a bedside heuristic, but should not be presented as ELAN criteria. CATALYST IPDMA (Dehbi et al., Lancet 2025, n=5441 pooling ELAN/OPTIMAS/TIMING/START) broadly supports early initiation without excess sICH.

antithrombotic.name: OPTIMAS
antithrombotic.question: Is early DOAC initiation after AF stroke non-inferior to standard timing?
antithrombotic.design: Pragmatic open-label RCT in UK, n>3500, early (≤4 days) vs standard (7-14 days)
antithrombotic.outcomes: Primary composite (ischemic stroke, ICH, unclassified stroke, systemic embolism at 90d): non-inferiority met. Similar safety.
antithrombotic.bottomLine: Confirms ELAN — earlier AC start is safe and at least as effective as delayed start.
antithrombotic.teachingPoint: ELAN + OPTIMAS have shifted practice toward earlier AC. Most centers now start within 48h-7d depending on stroke severity.

antithrombotic.name: CAPRIE
antithrombotic.question: Is clopidogrel superior to aspirin for secondary prevention of vascular events?
antithrombotic.design: RCT, n=19,185, clopidogrel 75 mg vs ASA 325 mg
antithrombotic.outcomes: Annual vascular events: 5.32% vs 5.83%, RRR 8.7% (narrow). Stroke subgroup: no significant difference.
antithrombotic.bottomLine: Clopidogrel modestly better than ASA overall; for ischemic stroke specifically the benefit is small.
antithrombotic.teachingPoint: Clopidogrel and ASA are both acceptable first-line single antiplatelets for non-cardioembolic stroke. Patient factors (cost, GI bleeding history, concurrent PPI) often drive the choice.

antithrombotic.name: MATCH
antithrombotic.question: Does long-term clopidogrel+ASA reduce stroke recurrence vs clopidogrel alone?
antithrombotic.outcomes: Primary composite vascular events: no significant difference. Life-threatening bleeding: 2.6% vs 1.3%, NNH=78.
antithrombotic.bottomLine: Long-term DAPT after stroke is NOT effective and increases bleeding.
antithrombotic.teachingPoint: Along with CHARISMA and CHANCE/POINT time-course analyses, supports short-course DAPT (~21 days) rather than prolonged dual therapy in most minor stroke/TIA patients.

antithrombotic.name: SPS3
antithrombotic.question: Does DAPT (clopi+ASA) reduce recurrent lacunar stroke?
antithrombotic.design: RCT, n=3020, clopi+ASA vs ASA alone, mean 3.4 years
antithrombotic.outcomes: Recurrent stroke: HR 0.92, NS. Major hemorrhage: HR 1.97. Mortality: HR 1.52 (increased with DAPT).
antithrombotic.bottomLine: DAPT does NOT reduce lacunar stroke recurrence; increases bleeding and mortality.
antithrombotic.teachingPoint: Critical: DAPT is for large-artery and cardioembolic mechanisms; small vessel (lacunar) disease → SINGLE antiplatelet long-term.

antithrombotic.name: COMPASS
antithrombotic.question: Does rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + ASA reduce MACE in stable atherosclerotic disease?
antithrombotic.design: RCT, n=27,395, stable CAD or PAD
antithrombotic.outcomes: MACE: HR 0.76 (24% RRR). Stroke: HR 0.58. Major bleeding: HR 1.70 (no ICH increase).
antithrombotic.bottomLine: Low-dose rivaroxaban + ASA reduces stroke/CV events in polyvascular disease.
antithrombotic.teachingPoint: Consider in stable patients with CAD+PAD, CAD+cerebrovascular disease. Most benefit in polyvascular patients. AHA/ASA 2021 Class 2b option.

antithrombotic.name: RESTART
antithrombotic.question: Is it safe to RESTART antiplatelet after ICH?
antithrombotic.design: RCT, n=537, restart antiplatelet vs no antiplatelet after spontaneous ICH survivor
antithrombotic.outcomes: Recurrent ICH: 4% vs 9%, HR 0.51 (favoring restart). Ischemic events: reduced with restart.
antithrombotic.bottomLine: Restarting antiplatelet after ICH does NOT increase recurrent ICH and reduces ischemic events.
antithrombotic.teachingPoint: Practice changer — historically feared restarting antiplatelet after ICH; RESTART shows benefit. Especially important if ischemic events reasonably likely (CAD, prior stroke).

rehabilitation.name: AVERT
rehabilitation.question: Does very early (<24h) intensive mobilization improve stroke outcomes?
rehabilitation.design: RCT, n=2104, very early intensive vs usual care
rehabilitation.outcomes: Favorable outcome (mRS 0-2 at 3mo): 46% vs 50% — LESS favorable with very-early intensive. Dose-response: more frequent higher-intensity sessions worse.
rehabilitation.bottomLine: Very-early (<24h) HIGH-intensity mobilization is HARMFUL. Early mobilization is fine but not aggressively intensive in first 24h.
rehabilitation.teachingPoint: Counter-intuitive. Doesn't contradict early mobilization per se — just says don't push HIGH-dose mobilization in the first 24h. Short, frequent sessions remain standard.

rehabilitation.name: FOCUS
rehabilitation.question: Does fluoxetine improve motor recovery after stroke?
rehabilitation.design: RCT, n=3127, fluoxetine 20 mg vs placebo x 6 months
rehabilitation.outcomes: Modified Rankin at 6 mo: no significant difference. Fewer depression cases but more fractures with fluoxetine.
rehabilitation.bottomLine: Fluoxetine does NOT improve motor recovery after stroke.
rehabilitation.teachingPoint: Contradicts earlier FLAME trial (2011). FOCUS + AFFINITY + EFFECTS (all negative for motor recovery) have closed the door on SSRIs for recovery. Still first-line for post-stroke depression.

rehabilitation.name: AFFINITY
rehabilitation.question: Does fluoxetine improve functional recovery in Australasian populations?
rehabilitation.design: RCT, n=1280, fluoxetine vs placebo x 6 months
rehabilitation.outcomes: Functional outcome (mRS shift): no significant difference. Excess fractures, hyponatremia.
rehabilitation.bottomLine: Confirms FOCUS — fluoxetine does NOT improve motor/functional outcomes.
rehabilitation.teachingPoint: Treat post-stroke depression with SSRI if clinically indicated (sertraline often preferred). Do NOT use SSRI expecting motor recovery benefit.

rehabilitation.name: SAVE
rehabilitation.question: Does CPAP reduce CV events in moderate-severe OSA?
rehabilitation.design: RCT, n=2717, CPAP vs usual care in established CV disease with OSA
rehabilitation.outcomes: Primary composite CV endpoint: no significant difference.
rehabilitation.bottomLine: CPAP does not reduce CV events in moderate-severe OSA without daytime sleepiness.
rehabilitation.teachingPoint: Doesn't mean don't treat OSA. Post-stroke OSA is common (50-70%), worsens neurologic function, treatment improves sleep quality, may improve BP. But SAVE tempers expectations for CV event reduction.

sahCvt.name: ISAT
sahCvt.question: Coiling vs clipping for ruptured aneurysms?
sahCvt.bottomLine: Coiling superior to clipping for most aneurysms (when both feasible).
sahCvt.teachingPoint: Shifted practice to endovascular-first for most ruptured aneurysms. Clipping still preferred for some MCA, wide-necked, or complex aneurysms.

sahCvt.name: Nimodipine (BANT + meta-analyses)
sahCvt.citation: BANT Br J Neurosurg 1989; Pickard BMJ 1989; Dorhout Mees Cochrane 2007
sahCvt.question: Does nimodipine improve outcomes in aSAH?
sahCvt.design: BANT (British Aneurysm Nimodipine Trial, 1989, n=554) + Cochrane meta-analyses
sahCvt.outcomes: Poor outcome (mRS 3-6 at 3mo): RR 0.67 favoring nimodipine. No definite effect on angiographic vasospasm.
sahCvt.bottomLine: Oral nimodipine 60 mg q4h × 21 days is Class 1 standard of care for all aSAH.
sahCvt.teachingPoint: Mechanism not solely vasospasm prevention — neuroprotective effect independent of large-vessel vasospasm. If hypotension occurs: split to 30 mg q2h rather than stopping.

sahCvt.name: SAHIT
sahCvt.question: Prognostic model for aSAH outcomes
sahCvt.outcomes: Validated model using age, WFNS, Fisher, hypertension, aneurysm size, treatment method.
sahCvt.bottomLine: Externally validated score for outcome prediction.
sahCvt.teachingPoint: Use with caution — communicates prognosis probabilistically, not deterministically. Patients occasionally do dramatically better or worse than predicted.
